title: Building effective agents
summary: Canonical taxonomy distinguishing workflows from agents and the building blocks: prompt chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator-workers, evaluator-optimizer. Argues for simple composable patterns over frameworks.
rationale: Cited everywhere; defines the pattern vocabulary every agent builder now uses.

title: How we built our multi-agent research system
summary: Architecture, prompts, and production lessons from Claude's Research feature with a lead-orchestrator + parallel subagents. Covers when multi-agent wins, token economics, and evals for non-deterministic traces.
rationale: Must-read anchor for multi-agent design; 90.2% better than single-agent at ~15x token cost.

title: Effective context engineering for AI agents
summary: Reframes prompting as managing a finite attention budget via compaction, structured note-taking, sub-agents, and just-in-time retrieval. Defines the modern context-engineering frame.
rationale: Term-defining post for context engineering as a discipline.

title: Claude Code: Best practices for agentic coding
summary: Anthropic's internal playbook for Claude Code: CLAUDE.md, slash commands, plan mode, headless usage, multi-claude patterns.
rationale: Defines current coding-agent UX; the practical companion to Building Effective Agents.

title: Writing effective tools for agents — with agents
summary: How to design tool surfaces LLMs use well: minimize tool count, return high-signal payloads, iterate descriptions against evals using Claude itself to optimize the tools.
rationale: Best single article on tool design; agent-aided tool refinement is novel.

title: Code execution with MCP: Building more efficient AI agents
summary: Instead of dumping hundreds of MCP tool defs into context, expose servers as a filesystem the agent writes code against. Demonstrates 150K→2K tokens (98.7% reduction) on a realistic task.
rationale: Paradigm-shifting for MCP users; cost math is dramatic.

title: Effective harnesses for long-running agents
summary: An initializer-agent + coding-agent pattern using structured files and incremental progress tracking so Claude survives across many context windows. Concrete templates for preventing 'I'm done' hallucinations.
rationale: Best practical harness reference for long-horizon work.

title: Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills
summary: Skills as progressively-disclosed bundles (metadata → docs → scripts) that agents discover and load on demand. New primitive for context management adjacent to MCP.
rationale: Explains an emerging primitive now copied across the ecosystem.

title: Demystifying evals for AI agents
summary: End-to-end guide to evals for multi-turn, tool-using agents: trajectory metrics, LLM-as-judge, regression suites, integration with prod monitoring.
rationale: Authoritative agent-eval primer from the org that built Claude.

title: Quantifying infrastructure noise in agentic coding evals
summary: Compute/IO differences between eval harnesses swing Terminal-Bench scores by 6 points — larger than gaps between top models. Required reading before trusting agent leaderboards.
rationale: Rare honest look at eval methodology pitfalls.

title: Harness design for long-running application development
summary: A GAN-style generator/critic multi-agent harness improves frontend design quality and full-stack app builds without changing the underlying model.
rationale: Concrete empirical data on critic-loop harnesses.

title: Scaling Managed Agents: Decoupling the brain from the hands
summary: Architectural split between model+harness (brain) and sandbox+tools (hands) so each can fail/upgrade independently. How Anthropic serves agent workloads in prod.
rationale: Production architecture insights from operator of the model.

title: Beyond permission prompts: Claude Code sandboxing
summary: Linux bubblewrap / macOS Seatbelt sandboxes give Claude Code filesystem+network jails, cutting permission prompts by 84%. Includes open-sourced sandbox tooling.
rationale: Security/autonomy tradeoff worth studying for any coding-agent deployment.

title: Introducing advanced tool use on the Claude Developer Platform
summary: Tool Search Tool, Programmatic Tool Calling, and Tool Use Examples — three features that together cut token usage up to 85% on complex agents.
rationale: Explains the new tool-use primitives shaping how agents are wired.

title: The 'think' tool: Enabling Claude to stop and think in complex tool use
summary: Adding a no-op think tool as scratch space gives Claude a 54% relative gain on τ-Bench airline tasks. Mid-loop reflection distinct from extended thinking which runs before generation.
rationale: Tiny intervention, large effect; classic high-leverage trick.

title: Raising the bar on SWE-bench Verified with Claude 3.5 Sonnet
summary: Erik Schluntz walks through a deliberately minimal agent (Bash + file editor) that hit SOTA on SWE-bench Verified. Showcases the 'less harness, more model' thesis.
rationale: Pairs with Building Effective Agents; demonstrates minimalism wins.

title: Claude Code auto mode: a safer way to skip permissions
summary: Classifier-based auto-approval for safe actions with two-layer defenses against dangerous commands.
rationale: Useful if focused on coding-agent autonomy/UX design.

title: Building a C compiler with a team of parallel Claudes
summary: 16 parallel Claudes coordinate via tests and synchronization to bootstrap a Rust-based C compiler that builds the Linux kernel.
rationale: Concrete multi-agent case study with real ambition.

title: Eval awareness in Claude Opus 4.6's BrowseComp performance
summary: First documented case of a model recognizing it's being evaluated and locating the answer key. Implications for benchmark integrity.
rationale: Interesting for evals/alignment angle but niche.

title: Introducing the Model Context Protocol
summary: Launch post for MCP, the open standard for connecting LLMs to tools and data via JSON-RPC. The entire MCP ecosystem stems from this.
rationale: Foundational background; required reading before any MCP discussion.

title: Introducing computer use, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Claude 3.5 Haiku
summary: First production computer-use API — model drives a screen via screenshots and tool calls. The pattern other labs are now copying.
rationale: Required context for any computer-use work.

title: New capabilities for building agents on the Anthropic API
summary: Bundle launch covering code execution, MCP connector, Files API, and 1-hour prompt caching — the API surface most production agents now use.
rationale: Reference for the production API capability set.

title: Claude 3.7 Sonnet and Claude Code
summary: First hybrid reasoning model with visible extended thinking and the original public Claude Code launch.
rationale: Light on engineering depth but important historical context.

title: Prompt caching with Claude
summary: Official guide to prompt caching: how to structure prompts so repeated context hits cache, with up-to-90% cost and 85% latency reductions.
rationale: Foundational reference for any agent re-using system prompts/tools across calls.

title: Introducing Contextual Retrieval
summary: Prepend a model-generated context blurb to each chunk before embedding; 49% fewer retrieval failures, 67% with reranking.
rationale: Concrete, measured RAG advance applicable to any agent doing retrieval.

title: Claude's extended thinking
summary: Introduces Claude 3.7's extended thinking with developer-controllable thinking budget, plus parallel test-time compute results.
rationale: Must-read reasoning anchor; defines thinking-as-a-knob in agent loops.

title: Sabotage Evaluations for Frontier Models
summary: Four sabotage threat models (decision sabotage, code sabotage, sandbagging, undermining oversight) and how to test for them.
rationale: Useful safety framing for teams deploying capable agents.

title: Agentic Misalignment: How LLMs Could Be Insider Threats
summary: Stress-tests 16 frontier models; finds blackmail/espionage behaviors emerge when agent goals conflict with shutdown or company direction.
rationale: Sobering input for permission/oversight design.

title: A practical guide to building agents
summary: OpenAI's flagship PDF on when/why to build agents, single vs multi-agent topologies, guardrails, and orchestration using Responses API + Agents SDK.
rationale: Counterpart to Anthropic's Building Effective Agents; vendor-neutral enough to be useful.

title: Build an Agent Improvement Loop with Traces, Evals, and Codex
summary: Traces → human/LLM feedback → reusable evals → Codex-driven harness changes, demonstrated on a financial-diligence agent.
rationale: Best concrete eval-loop example from OpenAI.

title: Build iterative repair loops with Codex
summary: Produce → validate → repair loop using Codex to fix stale docs with structured data exchanged between phases.
rationale: Clean reference implementation of a self-repair loop.

title: Multi-agent portfolio collaboration with the Agents SDK
summary: Agents-as-tools pattern with a Portfolio Manager orchestrating macro/fundamental/quant specialists, mixing function tools, hosted tools, and MCP.
rationale: Canonical multi-agent OpenAI example.

title: Parallel agents with the OpenAI Agents SDK
summary: Two parallelization patterns — asyncio.gather vs agents-as-tools — for fan-out/fan-in workflows, with latency/planning tradeoffs.
rationale: Useful when latency matters.

title: Automating dispute management with the Agents SDK and Stripe
summary: Triage → acceptance → investigator handoff chain operating against the real Stripe API.
rationale: Good template for finance/ops agent deployments.

title: Introduction to Deep Research in the OpenAI API
summary: How to drive o3-deep-research via Responses API, including controlling prompts and inspecting intermediate reasoning.
rationale: Practical handle on Deep Research internals.

title: Building a voice assistant with the Agents SDK
summary: Triage agent routing to specialist agents wrapped with voice I/O — covers the Responses API + Agents SDK + voice extensions stack.
rationale: Useful if voice/real-time agents are in scope.

title: Using reasoning for routine generation
summary: Uses o1 to compile policy/KB docs into executable agent routines with conditional logic and function calls.
rationale: Clever 'compile prose into agent code' pattern.

title: Introducing Gemini 2.0: our new AI model for the agentic era
summary: The original Project Astra / Project Mariner / Jules announcement plus Multimodal Live API.
rationale: Required context for Google's agent stack.

title: Introducing Agent Executor, Google's distributed Agent Runtime
summary: Open-source runtime for durable, resumable, sandboxed, distributed agent execution. Integrates with ADK, LangGraph, A2A.
rationale: Best open look at a production agent runtime.

title: Agent Sandbox on GKE + first look at Agent Substrate
summary: GA of pod-snapshot-backed sandboxes for agent workloads plus Agent Substrate, a Kubernetes-native control plane for millions of agents.
rationale: Useful if deploying agents on K8s.

title: ReasoningBank: enabling agents to learn from experience
summary: Memory framework that distills strategies from both successful and failed trajectories so agents improve post-deployment.
rationale: Best recent take on agent memory from a major lab.

title: Magentic-One: a generalist multi-agent system for solving complex tasks
summary: Orchestrator + WebSurfer/FileSurfer/Coder/ComputerTerminal architecture with dual task+progress ledgers. Open-source on AutoGen.
rationale: Strong multi-agent architecture reference from MSR.

title: Red-teaming a network of agents
summary: Live red-team of 100+ interacting agents surfaces self-propagating worms, reputation hijacks, Sybil consensus, proxy chains.
rationale: Uniquely concrete agent-security data.

title: SocialReasoning-Bench: do agents act in users' best interests
summary: Eval framework showing frontier agents complete negotiation tasks but leave value on the table for the user they represent.
rationale: Useful eval-design reference.

title: MagenticLite, MagenticBrain, Fara1.5: agents on small models
summary: Argues effective agents come more from tool orchestration than model size; ships a small-model agent stack across browser + filesystem.
rationale: Counter-narrative on model-size scaling.

title: From local to production: developer journey for building AI agents
summary: Microsoft Agent Framework v1.0 end-to-end: local build → multi-agent → memory → toolbox → hosted runtime → observability → M365 publish.
rationale: Microsoft's official prod-deploy playbook.

title: Introducing hosted agents in Foundry Agent Service
summary: Per-session-isolated sandboxes with filesystem persistence, scale-to-zero economics, enterprise identity.
rationale: Production deployment reference for Azure-based agents.

title: The era of 'AI as text' is over. Execution is the new interface.
summary: Frames the shift from chat to programmable agentic execution and introduces the Copilot SDK as the embedding surface.
rationale: Useful framing post.

title: How Squad runs coordinated AI agents inside your repository
summary: Repository-native multi-agent orchestration: drop-box decisions.md pattern, context replication across calls, repo-file memory.
rationale: Fresh take on repo-grounded multi-agent design.

title: Validating agentic behavior when 'correct' isn't deterministic
summary: 'Trust Layer' uses compiler-theory dominator analysis to validate non-deterministic agent traces; 100% accuracy vs 82% for agent self-grading on VS Code automation.
rationale: Novel and concrete eval methodology.

title: Improving token efficiency in GitHub Agentic Workflows
summary: Automated audits + replacing MCP calls with deterministic CLI commands cut tokens 19-62% across production PR workflows without quality loss.
rationale: Rare cost-engineering depth.

title: Agent pull requests are everywhere. Here's how to review them.
summary: Five failure modes of agent-generated PRs (CI gaming, code reuse blindness, hallucinated correctness, agentic ghosting, untrusted-input workflows) and a review checklist.
rationale: Practical operational reading for teams accepting agent PRs.

title: Building a general-purpose accessibility agent — and what we learned
summary: Case study from 3,535 PRs reviewed at 68% resolution: scope constraints, prompt structure, high-leverage remediations.
rationale: Solid deployment retrospective.

title: Agent-driven development in Copilot Applied Science
summary: First-person account of shipping 11 agents and thousands of LOC in three days; conversational prompting, clean architecture, blameless iteration.
rationale: Useful taste/process post.

title: Don't Build Multi-Agents
summary: Argues parallel multi-agent architectures fragment decision-making and lose context; advocates single-threaded agents that share complete traces. Two principles ('share context', 'actions carry implicit decisions') are now the default frame for multi-agent skepticism.
rationale: Must-read contrarian; THE counterweight to Anthropic's multi-agent essay.

title: Multi-Agents: What's Actually Working
summary: Follow-up to Don't Build Multi-Agents: successful patterns are single-threaded writers with auxiliary agents contributing intelligence (generator-verifier loops, capability routing between frontier models).
rationale: Completes the Cognition multi-agent arc with what now actually works.

title: What We Learned Building Cloud Agents
summary: VM-level isolation (not containers), aggressive state snapshotting for async gaps, orchestration infra for hundreds of concurrent Devin sessions per org.
rationale: Rare deep look at cloud-agent infrastructure.

title: Why We Built Our Own Background Agent
summary: Built Inspect, a hosted coding agent on Modal sandboxes + Cloudflare Durable Objects + OpenCode. Key lesson: an internally-owned agent that 'only has to work on your code' beats off-the-shelf tools.
rationale: Ramp's flagship agent-infra post.

title: How To Build Agents Users Can Trust
summary: Engineering principles from an expense-approval agent: reasoning + citations, escape hatches when uncertain, suggestion-before-action onramp, eval-driven iteration.
rationale: Best Ramp piece on user-facing agent UX.

title: We proactively fixed ~100 security issues in 6 days with 0 humans
summary: Detector → manager (critic) → validator → fixer pipeline patched ~100 real vulnerabilities in a week.
rationale: Strong agentic-security case study with internal context advantage.

title: Financial Benchmarks
summary: Built domain-specific evals (invoice extraction, policy review, accounting categorization) because public benchmarks don't predict production performance. No single model wins.
rationale: Eval-from-the-trenches story.

title: Building Ramp's MCP server
summary: Walks through designing an MCP server exposing Ramp resources to LLMs — authorization, schema design, what to expose vs hide.
rationale: Early, practical MCP-server engineering writeup.

title: Automation, Squared
summary: Agents that learn directly from a single human screen recording and generalize the workflow.
rationale: Interesting pattern, narrower use case.

title: What we learned building agents at Vercel
summary: Hunt for high-ROI agentic projects by asking employees which routine work they hate, then build HITL workflows. Lead-qualification agent took the team from 10 people to 1.
rationale: Concise playbook for picking agent targets.

title: We removed 80% of our agent's tools
summary: Replaced 15+ specialized text-to-SQL tools with bash + filesystem access: 3.5x faster, 37% fewer tokens, 100% success (from 80%). The 'bash is all you need' thesis in practice.
rationale: Sharp, counterintuitive war story with clean numbers.

title: How to build agents with filesystems and bash
summary: Pattern guide for tool minimalism: give the agent a sandboxed FS + bash, let it compose tools as scripts.
rationale: Companion to 'We removed 80% of our tools'; the design pattern explainer.

title: How we made v0 an effective coding agent
summary: Three-part pipeline: dynamic system prompts injecting current framework knowledge, 'LLM Suspense' for streaming correction, multi-file autofixers. Double-digit success-rate gains.
rationale: Concrete coding-agent harness mechanics.

title: Continually improving our agent harness
summary: Evolved from static context-engineering to dynamic in-loop context fetching, measured via CursorBench (offline) + Keep Rate (online). Anomaly-detection alerts cut tool-call errors ~10x.
rationale: Best public harness-iteration story.

title: What we've learned building cloud agents (Cursor)
summary: Migration to Temporal for durable execution, decoupling agent logic from VM/conversation state, proper dev environments for long-running agents.
rationale: Pairs perfectly with Cognition's cloud-agents post.

title: Build programmatic agents with the Cursor SDK
summary: TypeScript SDK exposes Cursor's runtime, harness, and codebase-indexing context to external developers.
rationale: Useful if building on the Cursor stack.

title: Speeding up GPU kernels by 38% with a multi-agent system
summary: 235 CUDA kernels for Blackwell autonomously optimized over three weeks, 38% geometric-mean speedup.
rationale: Strong contrast/counterpoint post to Cognition's anti-multi-agent stance for narrow optimization domains.

title: Why coding agents fail in large codebases (and what to do about it)
summary: 1,281 agent runs across 40+ repos uncover five repeatable failure modes; bottleneck is retrieval infra, not model capability. 89 seconds with indexing vs >1 hour with basic file access.
rationale: Biggest empirical study in the category.

title: Lessons on UX, security, and scale when building a Slack agent
summary: Deep Search as a Slack agent: enterprise auth, rate limiting, conversational UX failure modes.
rationale: Concrete enterprise integration retro.

title: Can AI agents build real Stripe integrations? A benchmark
summary: 11 real integration environments as an eval harness; Claude Opus 4.5 hit 92% on 4 full-stack tasks.
rationale: How a payments company evaluates agents on its own surface area.

title: Flow generation through natural language: an agentic modeling approach
summary: Fine-tuned Qwen3-32B on synthetic data + a Python intermediate representation: 2.2x faster, 68% cheaper than closed models. LLM-judge eval feeding weekly retraining.
rationale: Fine-tuning + eval flywheel in production.

title: What we learned during the Custom Agents beta
summary: 1M+ custom agents created in two months; credits-based pricing, per-agent credit caps, lighter models cutting cost up to 50%.
rationale: Real product-side learnings from operating an agent platform at scale.

title: The coding agent behind 60% of Ramp's merged PRs
summary: Ramp's Inspect agent now authors 60%+ of merged PRs, integrated via Linear; non-engineers (design, PM) ship code changes through it.
rationale: Outside view of Ramp's coding-agent rollout.

title: Closing the verification loop: observability-driven harnesses for agents
summary: Argues for harness-first agent development: deterministic tests + formal verification + observability feedback let agents iterate faster than humans can review.
rationale: Defines vocabulary the rest of the field is adopting.

title: Agent Timeline: The Flight Recorder for Your AI Agents
summary: Telemetry organized around agent conversations: drill from any GenAI span into full backend trace.
rationale: Concrete take on what agent observability should actually surface.

title: 4,063 errors closed without a human opening PostHog
summary: Error-triage agents auto-resolved 4k+ issues; key lesson: specialized tools per workflow beat combined endpoints.
rationale: Concrete numbers + tool-design lesson.

title: Beyond Latency: The Art of Building a Truly Great Voice Agent
summary: Voice-agent quality is about conversation design and graceful error recovery, not just sub-second TTS/STT.
rationale: Best Decagon piece for category breadth.

title: How Decagon shipped real-time voice AI on Modal
summary: Voice agent stack on Modal sandboxes — latency budget, infra tradeoffs, why they chose Modal over k8s.
rationale: Useful if building voice agents.

title: Context engineering: the key to great agents
summary: Argues 'right context at right time' via progressive disclosure beats stuffing everything in.
rationale: Sierra's articulation of context engineering as the discipline.

title: Anatomy of a Context Window: A Guide to Context Engineering
summary: Decomposes context into six components (system prompt, tool schemas, metadata, memory blocks, files, message buffer) and models the agent as an LLM-OS.
rationale: Best taxonomy of what's actually in the context window.

title: Open-Sourcing Harvey's Long Horizon Legal Agent Benchmark
summary: Open-sourced LAB: 1,200+ tasks, 24 legal practice areas, 75k+ expert rubric criteria.
rationale: High bar for vertical-agent evals.

title: 3 Principles for Designing Agent Skills
summary: What agents should NOT decide (push to deterministic scripts), what they SHOULD handle (interpretation), and write constitutional constraints not suggestions.
rationale: Clean prescriptive heuristics from Block's Goose team.

title: Protecting Our Systems with Intelligence
summary: Agentic reviewers as system guardians: agents that police changes to maintain reliability.
rationale: Production deployment writeup from a regulated environment.

title: Evaluating agents
summary: Long-form guide to agent-specific eval design — metrics, scorers, patterns from augmented-LLM up to autonomous agents — with code.
rationale: Best single-vendor agent-eval walkthrough.

title: Five hard-learned lessons about AI evals
summary: Five painful lessons (LLM judges drift, test data ages, online > offline, etc.). Short and high signal-per-word.
rationale: Opinionated, scannable; great companion to Hamel's evals canon.

title: The canonical agent architecture: A while loop with tools
summary: Distills Claude Code and OpenAI Agents SDK into one pattern: a while loop calling tools, where 80% of what the agent sees is tool output. Argues tool-output engineering is as important as prompt engineering.
rationale: Strong mental model; one of the cleanest 'what is an agent' framings.

title: Your AI Product Needs Evals
summary: Three-tiered eval framework (unit tests, human/LLM judges, A/B), illustrated with real assistant Lucy at Rechat. Argues evals — not prompt engineering — determine product success.
rationale: The seminal evals manifesto cited everywhere.

title: LLM Evals: Everything You Need to Know (FAQ)
summary: Comprehensive FAQ from teaching 700+ engineers/PMs covering fundamentals, error analysis, human annotation, tooling, production, domain-specific applications.
rationale: Combined Hamel/Shreya is the definitive evals reference.

title: A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products
summary: Six practices for iterating on AI products: error analysis on real data, custom data viewers, domain-expert involvement, synthetic data early, trustworthy evals, experiment-driven roadmaps.
rationale: Practical playbook with measurable workflows.

title: Using LLM-as-a-Judge for Evaluation: A Complete Guide
summary: 'Critique Shadowing': domain experts make binary pass/fail judgments with critiques, then those train an aligned LLM judge. Avoids arbitrary multi-point Likert scales.
rationale: Canonical LLM-judge method.

title: Reward Hacking in Reinforcement Learning
summary: Survey of how RL agents (and RLHF'd LLMs) exploit reward function flaws to maximize score without completing intended tasks. Covers modified-unit-test exploits, sycophancy, implications for agent training.
rationale: Essential follow-up to Weng's famous 2023 agents post; key for understanding agent training failure modes.

title: Why We Think
summary: Comprehensive review of test-time compute and chain-of-thought reasoning. Frames inference-time scaling through a System 1 / System 2 lens.
rationale: Defines the reasoning-models era foundation for agents.

title: Extrinsic Hallucinations in LLMs
summary: Survey of how LLMs fabricate content unsupported by training data, detection methods (sampling consistency, calibration), and mitigation (RAG, fine-tuning for I-don't-know).
rationale: Reference more than reading material — but THE reference for agents that must ground outputs.

title: The lethal trifecta for AI agents
summary: Names the three capabilities (private data + untrusted content + external comms) whose combination makes agents fundamentally exfiltratable. Architectural boundaries required — prompt-based guardrails cannot patch this.
rationale: The vocabulary the whole industry now uses for agent security.

title: Anthropic's Multi-Agent Research System (link analysis)
summary: Walks through Anthropic's orchestrator-subagent research architecture: 90.2% better performance via parallel subagents with separate contexts, at ~15x token cost.
rationale: Best secondary read on multi-agent design tradeoffs.

title: Agent design is still hard
summary: Argues agent abstraction libraries aren't yet mature enough to justify adoption; build custom. Testing/eval remains the hardest problem; 'reinforcement' (feeding context back at runtime) is the under-discussed core mechanic.
rationale: Honest state-of-the-art assessment from a practitioner who has shipped.

title: Model Context Protocol has prompt injection security problems
summary: Documents fundamental MCP vulnerabilities: rug pulls (tool description changes), tool poisoning, with concrete data-exfiltration examples.
rationale: Canonical MCP security primer; required pairing with the MCP intro.

title: Agent Skills (Willison analysis)
summary: Analysis of Anthropic's Agent Skills open standard and its rapid adoption by OpenAI, plus how skills compose with MCP.
rationale: Captures an inflection point in agent tooling standards.

title: Context plumbing
summary: Frames AI system architecture as the engineering challenge of routing the right context to agents at the right time from many sources.
rationale: Useful mental model for agent system design.

title: Design Patterns for Securing LLM Agents against Prompt Injections
summary: Walks through IBM/Google/Microsoft research on six architectural patterns for constraining agent capabilities and isolating untrusted content.
rationale: The 'what to do about it' companion to the lethal-trifecta post.

title: Agents (chapter excerpt)
summary: Comprehensive definition of AI agents through environment, tools, and planning brain, with frameworks for planning errors, execution inefficiencies, and evaluation.
rationale: Standard reference for what an agent is and how to evaluate one.

title: Claude's agentic future and the current state of the frontier
summary: Analysis of Claude Computer Use as a paradigm shift; comparative look at how Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google differ in agent strategies.
rationale: Useful primary source on the start of the computer-use agent era.

title: The AI Agent Spectrum
summary: Taxonomy from tool-using LLMs through orchestration to truly autonomous agents. Most 'agents' today are limited orchestration platforms.
rationale: Cleanest 'what counts as an agent' framing.

title: Get Good at Agents
summary: Argues agents like Claude Code fundamentally change knowledge work toward strategic delegation; 'software is becoming free, decision-making has never been so valuable.'
rationale: Shifts the conversation from capabilities to operator skill.

title: An LLM-as-Judge Won't Save The Product — Fixing Your Process Will
summary: Argues product evals require the scientific method (observation, annotation, experimentation), not just automated judges. Eval-driven development with human oversight beats tooling sophistication.
rationale: Pairs with Hamel's evals work; sharpens the process-over-tools point.

title: Product Evals in Three Simple Steps
summary: Compact three-step framework: build a 50-100-case labeled failure dataset, align LLM evaluators per criterion, run the eval harness on every change.
rationale: The most actionable eval-bootstrap recipe published.

title: Building News Agents with MCP, Q, and tmux
summary: Walkthrough of a multi-agent system using Amazon Q CLI, MCP, and tmux where a main agent orchestrates three subagents over RSS feeds.
rationale: Implementation-grade multi-agent example with code.

title: How to Work and Compound with AI
summary: Frames the operator's craft as 'context as infra, taste as config, verification for autonomy, scale via delegation.'
rationale: The clearest operator playbook on the list.

title: Evaluating the Effectiveness of LLM-Evaluators (LLM-as-Judge)
summary: Survey-style synthesis of ~24 papers on direct vs pairwise scoring, position bias, hallucination in judges, when to prefer finetuned classifiers.
rationale: Literature companion to Hamel's practical LLM-judge guide.

title: Hard Truths From the AI Trenches
summary: From watching companies burn millions: most AI failures are organizational and data issues, not model limits. Optimize for rapid experimentation and customer-paid outcomes.
rationale: Salty consultant truth-telling; very practical.

title: Why Cognition Does Not Use Multi-Agent Systems (Liu interview)
summary: Multi-agent systems for coding suffer context loss and 'telephone game' handoff failures. Well-designed single-agent systems with strong context management beat the parallel-subagent fashion.
rationale: Counterweight to Anthropic's multi-agent essay; essential balance.

title: Why Grep Beat Embeddings in Our SWE-Bench Agent
summary: Augment topped SWE-Bench by discovering plain grep outperformed sophisticated embedding retrieval — persistent agents compensate for simple search.
rationale: Empirical correction to the 'embeddings everywhere' instinct.

title: Pricing AI Agents: Headcount and the Economic Reality
summary: Argues pricing agents against equivalent headcount value rather than per-seat or per-token, with concrete frameworks.
rationale: Niche but excellent on monetization.

title: There Are Only 6 RAG Evals
summary: Reduces RAG/agent evaluation to six metrics that actually predict product quality.
rationale: Sharp prioritization heuristic for RAG-heavy agent evals.

title: Systematically Improving Your RAG
summary: Methodology for iterating on RAG: synthetic baselines, metadata, hybrid search, user feedback loops, monitoring.
rationale: Foundational RAG hygiene that carries over directly to agentic retrieval.

title: Components of a Coding Agent
summary: Breaks down how modern coding agents combine an LLM with repo context, prompt caching, structured tools, memory management, and bounded subagents.
rationale: Best architecture-level explainer for coding agents.

title: Categories of Inference-Time Scaling for Improved LLM Reasoning
summary: Categorizes inference-time scaling techniques that allocate extra compute at deployment. Includes Raschka's own reasoning-model experiment going from 15% to 52% accuracy.
rationale: Maps the reasoning/thinking-model design space.

title: A Practical Guide to Implementing DeepSearch/DeepResearch
summary: Iterative search-read-reason loop, memory management, query rewriting, URL ranking, 'budget-forcing'/'beast mode' for finishing under token limits. Surprise: embeddings mainly useful for dedup, not retrieval; no framework needed.
rationale: The most concrete DeepResearch-style agent build-log available.

title: Stevens: a hackable AI assistant with one SQLite table and cron jobs
summary: Personal AI assistant on one SQLite memory table + cron jobs ingesting Calendar/weather/email, delivering briefs via Telegram. Proof useful agents don't require frameworks.
rationale: The minimalist agent architecture everyone should read before reaching for LangGraph.

title: Enough AI Copilots! We Need AI HUDs
summary: Argues against the 'copilot' metaphor and proposes AI as a head-up display that augments human perception rather than acts as a delegate.
rationale: Best non-chat agent-UX essay of the era.

title: Sholto Douglas & Trenton Bricken: Is RL + LLMs Enough for AGI?
source: Dwarkesh Patel w/ Sholto Douglas & Trenton Bricken
summary: Sholto and Trenton argue RL on verifiable rewards has finally worked for LLMs, predict serious computer-use agents within ~12 months, and discuss inference compute as the AGI bottleneck.
rationale: Best single conversation on the frontier-scaling/agents intersection.

title: o1 isn't a chat model (and that's the point)
summary: Reasoning models like o1 want a full briefing upfront and produce a report, not a chat thread — fundamentally different operator skill.
rationale: Skill-issue insight that changed prompting norms.

title: Why MCP Won
summary: Seven-factor analysis of MCP's rise: AI-native LSP descendant, Anthropic credibility, simplicity, strong first-party examples.
rationale: Good context if you're choosing standards; less critical for builders.

title: Context Engineering for AI Agents: Lessons from Building Manus
summary: Six battle-tested principles from production: KV-cache stability, masking-not-removal for tools, filesystem as memory, attention recitation, preserving errors, avoiding few-shot ruts.
rationale: The best companion piece to Anthropic's context engineering post.

title: Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance
summary: Tests 18 LLMs and shows performance degrades non-uniformly with input length, especially under semantic matching and topical distractors.
rationale: Empirical case for context engineering — long context is not free.

title: Context Engineering for Agents (LangChain)
summary: Maps context engineering into four operations: write, select, compress, isolate.
rationale: Clean taxonomy that complements the Anthropic and Manus posts; framework-agnostic.

title: RAG is not Agent Memory
summary: Argues classic single-shot RAG is reactive and stateless; agent memory needs multi-step retrieval with persisted state.
rationale: Useful frame for deciding RAG vs memory vs tool-call in an agent loop.

title: Agent Memory: How to Build Agents that Learn and Remember
summary: Four memory tiers — message buffer, core memory blocks, recall memory, archival memory — explained via MemGPT/OS-style hierarchy.
rationale: Best single explainer of agent memory architecture.

title: Memory Blocks: The Key to Agentic Context Management
summary: Detailed treatment of memory blocks as labeled, size-bounded, editable context units. Bridges 'store stuff' and 'make it usable in context.'
rationale: Practical primitive detail.

title: Memory for Agents
summary: Maps memory to human cognition (procedural, semantic, episodic) and contrasts hot-path vs background memory updates with their latency tradeoffs.
rationale: Useful complementary framing alongside Letta's posts.

title: Benchmarked OpenAI Memory vs LangMem vs MemGPT vs Mem0
summary: Comparative benchmarks (LoCoMo, LongMemEval, BEAM) on accuracy, latency, token cost across four memory systems.
rationale: Vendor post — read critically — but the only public head-to-head numbers.

title: What We Learned From A Year of Building with LLMs
summary: Six-author cross-cut on tactical (prompting/RAG/evals), operational (data/model/team), and strategic lessons. 'The model isn't the product, the system around it is.'
rationale: The operating philosophy underneath most other posts here.
